The acquisition of Enterprise AI is facing a critical turning point: 42% of large companies (1,000+ employees) have actively engaged AI, yet 40% remain stuck in exploration and experimentation and cannot capture competitive advantages that are already emerging from early movers.

The gap is widening: 59% of companies already using or researching AI have accelerated investment over the past 24 months, with China (85%), India (74%) and the UAE (72%) showing the leading acceleration, while Canada (35%), Australia (38%) and the United Kingdom (40%) are clearly slowing down.
Financial services are leaders in industry, with about half of the companies active in the telecommunications sector, compared to 37%.

The most common use cases are IT process automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%), AI governance and monitoring (25%), suggesting that AI includes not only peripheral functions but also core operations.
Three obstacles are responsible for most blocked implementations: limited AI competence and expertise (33%), excessive data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
Data protection (57%) and trust and transparency (43%) are the dominant blockers especially for generative AI.
The personnel dimension leads to a problem: one in five organisations lacks employees with skills to use AI tools, 16% cannot fill this gap, and only 34% are employees who actively retrain.

The confidence deficit is equally large: while 85% of IT professionals agree that consumers prefer companies with transparent AI practices and 83% declare that their business needs to be explained, less than half have taken concrete steps towards trusted AI – only 27% reduce bias, 37% track data progress and 44% develop ethical AI policies.

Organisations still in the exploration phase must immediately prioritize the closure of the skills gap through structured reskilling programs – the 39% of AI deployment companies already investing in human resource development show that:

This is executable since accessible tools and embedded AI in off-the-shelf applications (with 45% and 37% of users respectively) have already eliminated the biggest technical barriers to access, leaving human capital as the most important remaining limitation to use.
